explanation: The requirement is to provide an explanation if an unexpected obstacle is encountered. This can be captured by the LTL formula G(unexpectedObstacle -> explain), which globally ensures that whenever 'unexpectedObstacle' occurs, 'explain' must also occur (either at the same moment or afterwards, depending on how 'explain' is defined). This property directly maps to the intent because it enforces an explanation for every unexpected obstacle/event.

The LTL formula captures the requirement that whenever an unexpected obstacle occurs (i.e., the atomic proposition 'unexpectedObstacle' becomes true), the system should provide an explanation (i.e., 'explain' should eventually be true). This is a typical response pattern: on every occurrence of 'unexpectedObstacle', there is a guarantee that 'explain' eventually follows. The property does not specify a limit on how quickly the explanation must be provided, only that it must eventually occur after every unexpected obstacle. No numerical constraints are used, and only the provided atomic propositions and LTL operators are present.

The natural language states: 'Explain if unexpected obstacle.' This suggests that whenever an unexpected obstacle is encountered, an explanation should be provided. In LTL, this requirement can be represented by stating that globally, it is always the case that if 'unexpectedObstacle' occurs, then 'explain' must also occur (at the same moment or in the future depending on context). The most direct translation is with the release or implication operator: G (unexpectedObstacle -> explain). This formula ensures that at every point in time, encountering an unexpected obstacle triggers the provision of an explanation.

The LTL property captures the requirement that whenever an unexpected obstacle occurs, an explanation must be provided (explain must occur). This is formally represented by the property G(unexpectedObstacle -> F explain), which means globally, if 'unexpectedObstacle' holds at any step, then it is guaranteed that eventually (possibly immediately) 'explain' will hold. This directly expresses the requirement in natural language. There are no previous LTL properties to compare against.

The requirement 'Explain if unexpected obstacle' means that whenever an unexpected obstacle is encountered, the system should provide an explanation. In LTL, this is typically captured as 'globally, if unexpectedObstacle holds, then explain must also hold (possibly at the same time or immediately after depending on the interpretation of causality or synchronization). The proposed formula 'G(unexpectedObstacle -> explain)' ensures that at every moment in the system, if an unexpected obstacle is detected, an explanation must also be provided at that same step. This directly captures the intended requirement using only LTL operators and the given atomic propositions, and there are no previous LTL formulas to compare or correct.

The natural language states 'Explain if unexpected obstacle'. This means that any time an unexpected obstacle occurs, an explanation should be provided. In LTL, this is typically captured by the property that globally, whenever 'unexpectedObstacle' is true, 'explain' must also be true at the same time. This is encoded as G(unexpectedObstacle -> explain). This ensures that at all times, if an unexpected obstacle is encountered, an explanation will be provided.

The natural language request is to trigger an explanation when an unexpected obstacle is encountered. In LTL, this is captured by stating that globally, whenever 'unexpectedObstacle' is true, 'explain' must also be true at the same time, i.e., it is required to immediately explain when encountering an unexpected obstacle. This is expressed as G(unexpectedObstacle -> explain). This ensures that every time an unexpected obstacle occurs, an explanation is provided at that time. Other forms (e.g., allowing delayed explanations) would not directly fit the immediate interpretation of 'if' as 'whenever'.

The natural language specifies that if an unexpected obstacle is encountered, then an explanation should be provided. In LTL, this is commonly captured as 'globally, whenever unexpectedObstacle happens, eventually explain will happen', or G(unexpectedObstacle -> F explain). This ensures that after each occurrence of unexpectedObstacle, explain must occur at some point in the future. This property directly maps the conditional and causal structure of the input phrase.

The property 'G(unexpectedObstacle -> explain)' says that globally, whenever an unexpected obstacle is encountered, an explanation must be provided. This directly maps the requirement: if an unexpected obstacle (unexpectedObstacle) occurs, then an explanation (explain) must be triggered. There are no previous LTL properties to compare against here.

The natural language prompt requests that an explanation be provided if an unexpected obstacle is encountered. This can be interpreted in LTL as: whenever (globally) an unexpected obstacle occurs, it must be the case that explain is also true at that point or eventually thereafter. The property G(unexpectedObstacle -> F explain) means that at every moment, if an unexpected obstacle is encountered, then eventually an explanation must be provided. This captures the requirement that explanations follow every unexpected obstacle, and does not make assumptions about their timing, only that they must eventually follow.
